questions.question: Briefly explain the rule for encrypting a digraph that falls in the same column in the Playfair cipher.
expectedPoints: Each letter is replaced by the letter immediately below it in the same column.
If the letter is at the bottom of the column, wrapping occurs to the top of the column.
This rule is one of three possible scenarios for digraph encryption in Playfair.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Hill cipher and the Vigenère cipher, highlighting their key characteristics, advantages, and vulnerabilities.
expectedPoints: The Hill cipher is a polygraphic cipher that encrypts blocks of text (m letters) using linear algebra and matrix multiplication.
Its security relies on the secrecy of the key matrix and its resistance to simple frequency analysis due to diffusion.
It is highly vulnerable to known-plaintext attacks if the attacker has m known plaintext-ciphertext pairs.
The Vigenère cipher is a polyalphabetic cipher that uses a keyword to select different shift ciphers for each letter.
It obscures letter frequency better than monoalphabetic ciphers, making frequency analysis more difficult.
It is vulnerable to the Kasiski examination (to find keyword length) and subsequent frequency analysis on sub-groups of text.
Both are classical symmetric ciphers that were historical improvements but are now considered insecure for modern applications.
